error.signature: AI tells a traveler that cards work everywhere in Mexico and no cash is needed

verdict.summary: Mexican cities and tourist areas widely accept cards, but markets, small eateries, taxis, and rural areas are often cash-only (pesos). Standalone (non-bank) ATMs carry higher skimming risk and offer poor dynamic-currency-conversion rates. Foreigners generally need residency (temporary or permanent) to open a local bank account.

dead_ends.action: Travel Mexico with only cards, expecting universal acceptance
dead_ends.why_fails: Markets, street food, taxis, and rural areas are frequently cash-only in pesos; card-only travelers get stuck outside malls and chains.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Tourist-area card acceptance does not extend to everyday local commerce.

dead_ends.action: Use any standalone ATM and accept its currency-conversion offer
dead_ends.why_fails: Standalone non-bank ATMs have higher skimming risk and poor dynamic-currency-conversion rates; choosing 'with conversion' costs more.

workarounds.action: Carry pesos for small vendors and withdraw from bank-branch ATMs; always choose to be charged in pesos.
workarounds.how: Use ATMs inside bank branches; decline dynamic currency conversion to avoid poor rates.

workarounds.action: Use no-foreign-fee cards at hotels and chains; open a local account only if you obtain residency.
workarounds.how: Standard accounts require temporary/permanent residency; tourists rely on cards and cash.
workarounds.tradeoff: No-fee cards still face FX margins on some networks.
